content:
The chain rule for derivatives of composed functions appears in the work of **Leibniz** and **Euler** as part of the formalization of calculus—answering how sensitivity propagates through nested transformations.

In the 20th century, **automatic differentiation** research (e.g. **Wengert**, **Linnainmaa**) showed how to organize the chain rule on computation graphs efficiently.

**Rumelhart, Hinton and Williams (1986)** popularized **backpropagation** for neural networks, making the chain rule the practical engine of deep learning training.

Today every major ML framework applies reverse-mode chain rule billions of times per training step when computing parameter gradients for generative and discriminative models alike.

timeline.label: 17th–18th century
timeline.description: Calculus pioneers formalize derivatives of composed functions.

timeline.label: 1970s
timeline.description: Automatic differentiation theory organizes forward and reverse chain rule on graphs.
Backpropagation paper connects chain rule to multi-layer neural network training.

timeline.label: Modern ML frameworks
timeline.description: Reverse-mode autograd makes large-scale chain rule execution routine in PyTorch, JAX and TensorFlow.
